title: SIGILL (ud2) crash in coroutine actor for fork/join with large generated designs
body:
## Environment

| Component | Version |
|-----------|---------|
| Verilator | 5.020 (Debian 5.020-1) |
| GCC | 13.3.0 (Ubuntu 13.3.0-6ubuntu2~24.04.1) |
| OS | Ubuntu 24.04, Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86-64 |
| Compilation flags | `--binary --timing --unroll-count 1024` (default `-Os`) |

## Description

When a SystemVerilog testbench uses `fork/join` with two concurrent tasks and the DUT is a large design with extensive generate blocks and many BRAM instances, the compiled binary crashes immediately with SIGILL before any simulation output.

GDB shows the crash at a `ud2` instruction in the coroutine `.actor` function for the initial block containing the `fork/join`. The `ud2` is placed after the `VlCoroutine` destructor call at the end of the `__Vtiming__0__1` function.

This may be related to #5906 (fork coroutine heap-use-after-free, fixed in 5.034), which is in the same fork/join coroutine codegen area but manifests differently (SIGSEGV/heap-use-after-free vs SIGILL/ud2 here). This bug is also size-dependent, unlike #5906.

## Testbench pattern that triggers the crash

```systemverilog
initial begin
    // ... setup ...
    fork
        feed_task();     // feeds data to DUT
        capture_task();  // captures outputs from DUT
    join
    // ... verification ...
    $finish;
end
```

## Generated C++ (simplified)

```cpp
VlCoroutine __Vtiming__0__1(Root* vlSelf) {
    __Vfork_1__0(vlSelf);  // launches fork branch 0
    __Vfork_1__1(vlSelf);  // launches fork branch 1
    // implicit co_return -> final_suspend -> ud2 placed after destructor
}
```

The function is a coroutine (returns `VlCoroutine`) but contains NO suspension points (`co_await`/`co_yield`/`co_return`). The fork branches are called as regular functions, creating `VlCoroutine` temporaries that are immediately discarded. GCC (at all optimization levels including `-O0`) inserts `ud2` after the implicit `co_return` and execution reaches it.

## Key observations

1. Crash occurs even with `-O0` (not an optimization bug).
2. Only triggers with certain DUT sizes — specifically a parameterized design where `BATCH_SIZE=8` produces many large generate blocks and ~30 BRAM instances. The same testbench works with `BATCH_SIZE=16`, `32`, `64`, or `128`.
3. A different testbench exercising the same `BATCH_SIZE=8` DUT with `fork/join` (but smaller local storage arrays) works fine.
4. Crash happens before any `$display` output — at the very start of simulation.

## GDB backtrace

```
Thread 1 "Vtb_inv_bridge_" received signal SIGILL, Illegal instruction.
0x000055555566ae04 in Vtb_inv_bridge_throughput___024root___eval_initial__TOP__Vtiming__0__1(Vtb_inv_bridge_throughput___024root*) ()
=> 0x55555566ae04: ud2
#0  ... __Vtiming__0__1 ...
#1  ... __Vtiming__0 [clone .actor] ()
#2  std::__n4861::coroutine_handle<void>::resume() const ()
```

## Workaround

Replace `fork/join` with an `always` block for output capture, avoiding the coroutine codegen entirely:

```systemverilog
// Instead of fork/join, use always block for capture:
logic capture_enable;
always @(posedge clk) begin
    if (capture_enable && dut_valid) begin
        results[count] <= dut_data;
        count <= count + 1;
    end
end

initial begin
    capture_enable = 1;
    feed_task();
    // wait for capture...
end
```

## Suspected root cause

The `fork/join` wrapper coroutine (`__Vtiming__0__1`) has no suspension points — the fork branches are launched as regular function calls returning `VlCoroutine` temporaries. GCC's coroutine transformation may be generating an invalid state machine for this degenerate case, or the coroutine frame size may interact with the large DUT to produce an out-of-bounds switch case. The size-dependent nature suggests the generated code structure (number of signals, NBA evaluation paths) affects the coroutine transformation.

I can provide a full reproducer design if needed — the DUT is a parameterized 256-bit modular arithmetic pipeline with ~300 DSP48E2 instances and extensive BRAM storage.
